title: Acquiring credentials when signals don't matter: Employers' support of employees who pursue postsecondary vocational degrees
abstract: Previous research has established that employers respond to postsecondary vocational degrees when hiring employees. Because employers lack information on the productive capacity of new employees, these credentials may signal preexisting capacities, rather than the acquisition of new skills. Employers' support for participation in vocational education programs by current employees, whose productive capacity is directly observable, suggests that the programs enhance participants' skills in ways that employers reward. This study found a substantial level of employers' support for employees' participation in vocational education. This support is as closely associated with the skills that employers want to enhance as it is to characteristics of either employees or jobs. The findings provide further evidence of the importance of subbaccalaureate vocational degrees.
